top_categories.workout_context: 79ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
At Knysna Cycle Tour, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Knysna Cycle Tour's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before July.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Knysna Cycle Tour. Train it until it feels boring.
Knysna Cycle Tour's mixed terrain forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 62 miles.
When someone attacks on mixed terrain, you have 10 seconds to respond or you're off the back. This builds that response.
62 miles on mixed terrain burns energy faster than the distance suggests. Train the fade resistance now.
Varied gradients on mixed terrain demand climbing versatility. Sit the shallow stuff, stand the steep stuff, switch without thinking.
Double-threshold training builds sustained power with less fatigue. More FTP per hour of training.
Low-cadence force work for 4,921ft of climbing. The slow, grinding ascents where you can't spin—you push.
Short, max efforts are unavoidable. Punchy climbs, gap closures, attacks on mixed terrain. If you haven't trained them, you'll crack.
How long can you hold 105% FTP before you blow? At Knysna Cycle Tour, that number decides whether you bridge or get dropped.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 40–60g carbs/hour for Knysna Cycle Tour’s 62 miles. Front-load calories in the first half. One bottle per hour minimum, more in heat.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on TT Threshold, Over Under, and Race Simulation to prepare you for 62 miles of mixed terrain in Knysna, Garden Route, South Africa.
